tweets.text:
Investors are piling into US equities at a record pace:

US equity ETFs have attracted +$880 billion in inflows year-to-date, on track for their largest annual inflow on record.

This already surpasses every full-year total except the all-time high set in 2025.

Furthermore, current inflows are more than twice the levels seen at the same point in 2021 and 2025.

At this rate, annual inflows will exceed +$1.4 trillion by year-end.

This would be ~$500 billion above the 2025 record of ~$920 billion.

Investor demand for US equities is unprecedented.

tweets.text:
The AI trade is diverging sharply:

The 30-day correlation between the largest US CapEx spenders and the semiconductor index, $SOX, is down to almost zero, near the lowest in at least 4.5 years.

This marks a sharp decline from the +0.78 positive correlation seen in April.

By comparison, the average correlation coefficient has been +0.60 since the start of 2022.

The divergence comes as semiconductor stocks have rallied while the largest AI infrastructure spenders have declined, and vice versa, since the beginning of June.

This tells us that investors are no longer treating AI infrastructure builders and chipmakers as one trade, as chipmakers benefit from AI demand while hyperscalers face questions over whether massive spending will generate sufficient returns.

The next phase of the AI trade will be defined by profitability, not investment.

The AI trade is diverging sharply:

The 30-day correlation between the largest US CapEx spenders and the semiconductor index, $SOX, is down to almost zero, near the lowest in at least 4.5 years.

This marks a sharp decline from the +0.78 positive correlation seen in April.

By comparison, the average correlation coefficient has been +0.60 since the start of 2022.

The divergence comes as semiconductor stocks have rallied while the largest AI infrastructure spenders have declined, and vice versa, since the beginning of June.

This tells us that investors are no longer treating AI infrastructure builders and chipmakers as one trade, as chipmakers benefit from AI demand while hyperscalers face questions over whether massive spending will generate sufficient returns.

The next phase of the AI trade will be defined by profitability, not investment.

tweets.text:
Travis Kalanick and Ben Horowitz on the cultural advantage to building in stealth:

"There's a humility thing to it, an anti-fame thing. Everybody in your company gets fulfillment from the work they do every day, not because they're gonna get famous."

"You start making decisions based on external validation versus internal correctness. I wanted to build without worrying about what The New York Times was saying."

@travisk @bhorowitz @eriktorenberg

tweets.text:
Investors are worried about Oracle's credit:

Oracle's, $ORCL, 5-year credit default swaps (CDS) are up to a record ~203 basis points.

This means protection against Oracle's default now costs ~$203,000 for every $10 million of principal.

Oracle's CDS have more than QUADRUPLED since mid-2025, even surpassing the 2008 Financial Crisis peak.

The company’s borrowing costs are also rising, with the spread on its 6.7% notes due 2056 widening +8 bps on Monday, to 263 bps, and its 5.7% notes due 2036 widening +9 bps, to 205 bps.

Meanwhile, S&P Global Ratings downgraded Oracle to BBB- on July 9th, just 1 notch above junk, citing its rapidly growing AI-related spending.

Oracle's credit risk is at crisis levels.

tweets.text:
Nothing you buy teleports to the store.

Over 70% of US freight moves by truck. Your groceries, your furniture, your meds, your dog's kibble, the parts in your car. A truck hauled every bit of it.

And rates per mile just ripped to the highest level on record.

That cost gets stapled onto every price tag in America, and you pay it at the register.

Watch the trucks. They price your inflation months before the Fed gets around to admitting it.

tweets.text: On this day in 1959, Vice President Richard Nixon and Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev argued about capitalism and communism while standing in a model American kitchen, a confrontation that became known as the Kitchen Debate. The setting was the American National Exhibition in Moscow, where the United States had built a full suburban house stocked with dishwashers, refrigerators, and labor-saving appliances to showcase the consumer abundance of a market economy. Nixon argued that the measure of a system was not its weapons but its ability to put a washing machine and a television in an ordinary worker's home. The exhibition drew nearly 3 million Soviet visitors, many seeing Pepsi, Polaroid cameras, and American cars for the first time. The consumer spending Nixon was selling that day now accounts for roughly 68% of all U.S. GDP.
tweets.full_text: On this day in 1959, Vice President Richard Nixon and Soviet Premier Nikita Khrushchev argued about capitalism and communism while standing in a model American kitchen, a confrontation that became known as the Kitchen Debate. Gold ran to nearly $5,600. Then it fell 28%.

Now $4,045, down 6% on the year.

The "debasement trade" everyone crowded into just got a reminder: fear doesn't go up in a straight line. When real yields rise, even the safe haven will fall.

Nothing is a one-way bet. Especially gold.

tweets.text:
Japan spent a record $74 billion in one month to prop up the yen.

It just hit a 40-year low anyway.

The lesson: intervention can't beat fundamentals. With rates near zero and debt over 200% of GDP, the yen keeps bleeding.

The yen carry trade is one of the biggest funding sources in global markets.

tweets.text:
🇨🇳 #China’s Local State Asset Managers Join Stock Rescue - Financial News

➡ A number of state-owned asset management companies affiliated with local governments stepped up stock purchases and dividend payout in an effort to support the market, Financial News says in a report.

➡ Beijing State-owned Capital Operation and Management Co. said it has poured nearly 10 billion yuan into the stock market, and will continue to increase stock holdings.

➡ Another state investment firm in Beijing pledged to buy more stocks, push for M&A between companies that it owns shares in and encourage them to pay higher cash dividend.

➡ The moves follow last week’s announcement by central government-managed state investors to increase stock purchases.

➡ State investment platforms in Shanghai and Shaanxi also announced market support measures.

➡ The coordinated push aims to bring more long-term capital into the market and strengthen its funding structure, rather than simply offset short-term volatility.

*Link (Chinese): https://t.co/NMoYqKhn41

$Meta is building this data center from scratch on ~1,000 acres of land land it bought from the City of El Paso in 2023 for $9M. 
El Paso Electric has about 2.9 GW of total generation capacity serving ~460K customers, so a 1 GW Meta campus is nearly the size of the entire existing utility system, which is why they're building dedicated gas generation. 
The site selection logic appears to be cheap land, proximity to Meta's existing campuses in New Mexico and Arizona (Los Lunas, Mesa), and the ability to negotiate custom power arrangements with a smaller regional utility rather than fighting ERCOT interconnection queues in the Dallas/Austin corridor.

The site selection logic appears to be cheap land, proximity to Meta's existing campuses in New Mexico and Arizona (Los Lunas, Mesa), and the ability to negotiate custom power arrangements with a smaller regional utility rather than fighting ERCOT interconnection queues in the Dallas/Austin corridor.

tweets.text:
THE DRAM/GPU/MW BARTER: TERMS INCOMING

South Korea's president is in Silicon Valley today.

His policy chief says 4+ GW of AI data center projects could be finalized during the visit.

Separate meetings with Jensen, Sam, Dario, and Hock Tan.

What's likely getting signed:
>HBM/DRAM supply agreements in exchange for GPU allocations (Samsung + SK Hynix → Nvidia + hyperscalers)
>Korean capital co-investing in US AI data centers. Powered land is part of the trade
>MOUs with named customers, sites, and Korean engineering partners

Korea brings capital + memory. 
US brings land, power, and GPU allocation.

Seoul announced this $880B investment plan last month. 
This trip will convert vague pledge into named projects.

tweets.text:
🚨🛢️🚢 Yesterday I showed you tanker captains messaging the Houthis through their AIS "Destination" field. Today I mapped all of them.
 
📡95 vessels right now are broadcasting crew/ownership instead of a port — "All Chinese Crew," "Armed Guards O/B," "China Crew & Owner" — the maritime version of hands up, don't shoot.

Nearly all of them: bunched in the Red Sea & Gulf of Aden. 
  
~4.9M DWT.  ~959k metric tons of cargo. 89% seen today.
 
From IMO<GO> on @TheTerminal
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
🚨🛢️🚢Wow — I just learned that in AIS ship-tracking data on @TheTerminal  you can watch crude oil tanker captains sending messages to the Houthis through the vessel's "Destination" field.

Instead of a port, they're broadcasting things like "China Crew & Owner," "Armed Guards https://t.co/9iod7kfvQy

This should be the ideal environment for dollar strength, yet it continues to struggle.

It appears fundamentally exhausted and poised for a major reversal — entirely consistent with its long-term cycle.

tweets.text:
The economy lives and dies by three sectors.

We call the components "Cyclical GDP"

The AI-capex cycle is coming through business equipment investment.

→ Computer equipment is just 8% of Cyclical GDP but has contributed to 144% of its growth rate over the last four quarters. https://t.co/hARxfF3RIV

Ignore 80% of GDP.

Groceries, healthcare...they barely move, even in recessions.

The cycle lives in 3 categories:

→ durable goods
→ housing
→ business equipment

I mapped it all in a free PDF.

Grab it with a sign-up for our free newsletter→ https://t.co/MJjGdBnPyb https://t.co/4SuyiP6c4B
